title: Gathering the Evidence
subtitle: Fieldwork, testing & working papers

hook.metaphor: A detective doesn't convict on a hunch — they gather evidence: documents, witness statements, samples, things they saw with their own eyes. Audit fieldwork is detective work. The auditor's golden rule is simple and ruthless: *if it isn't evidenced, it didn't happen.* A confident 'yes, we always do that' counts for nothing without proof.
hook.scenario: An auditor asks whether large payments get a second approval. The manager says, 'Absolutely, every time.' The auditor pulls 25 random payments to check. Twenty have the second approval; five don't. Whose account was right — and what just happened?

learn: **Fieldwork** is the evidence-gathering heart of an audit. Auditors get evidence several ways: **inspecting** documents, **observing** a process in action, **re-performing** a control themselves, and **inquiry** (asking people) — though inquiry alone is the *weakest* evidence, because people misremember and misstate (our 'every time' manager).

Because you can't check everything, auditors **sample** (Internal Controls Level 5): test a representative subset to conclude about the whole. Pulling 25 payments and finding 5 failures isn't a story about those 5 — it suggests a roughly 20% failure rate across all of them. That's the power of sampling.

Good evidence is **sufficient** (enough of it) and **appropriate** (relevant and reliable). A pile of weak evidence isn't sufficient; one strong document can outweigh many vague assurances. Auditors weigh quality, not just quantity.

Everything is recorded in **working papers** — the documented trail of what was tested, how, and what was found. They're the audit's evidence file: they let conclusions be reviewed, challenged, and stand up to scrutiny later. 'Trust me, I checked' is not an audit; the working papers are the proof that you did.

And the cardinal mindset is **professional scepticism**: not assuming people are lying, but not simply taking their word either. The auditor in our scenario didn't accuse the manager — they *verified*. That gap between 'I was told' and 'I tested' is the whole value of audit.
bridge: So at work, you can adopt the auditor's reflex on any important claim: *what's the evidence, and is it strong enough?* Treat 'we always do that' as a hypothesis to test, not a fact — and value the document, the sample, the re-performance over the confident assertion. 'If it isn't evidenced, it didn't happen' is a quietly powerful habit far beyond audit.

interactions.title: 'Every time' vs the sample
interactions.prompt: The manager swears second approvals always happen; a sample of 25 shows 5 missing. What's the takeaway?

options.label: Trust the manager — 5 is a tiny number.
options.feedback: 5 of 25 is ~20% failing — a serious control gap, not a rounding error. And inquiry ('every time') is the weakest evidence; the test beats the assertion.

options.label: The evidence overrides the assertion — test results reveal a real control failure.
options.feedback: Exactly. Professional scepticism plus sampling exposed what confident words hid. ~20% of large payments lacked the required approval — that's the finding, evidenced.

options.label: Re-do the audit until the numbers look better.
options.feedback: That's the opposite of audit — you don't massage evidence to a desired answer. The sample found a real issue; you report and fix it.

interactions.title: Strong or weak evidence?
interactions.prompt: Rank each source of audit evidence.

items.label: Re-performing the control yourself and seeing the result
A general verbal reassurance with no documents

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Audit is evidence-gathering — inspect, observe, re-perform, and sample rather than just take people's word — recorded in working papers, because if it isn't evidenced, it didn't happen.”

checkpoint.q: The weakest form of audit evidence is usually…
checkpoint.explain: People misremember and misstate; inquiry must be backed by stronger, verifiable evidence.

checkpoint.q: Finding 5 failures in a random sample of 25 payments suggests…
options: A roughly 20% failure rate across all such payments
checkpoint.explain: Sampling lets you infer the wider failure rate — that's a serious control gap, not 5 isolated cases.

options: Document what was tested, how, and what was found, so it can be reviewed
checkpoint.explain: They're the evidence trail proving the work was done and letting conclusions stand up to scrutiny.
